core_claim: NVIDIA's open Nemotron 3 Ultra is built for long-running agents with up to a million tokens of context, and open weights remove the vendor guardrail, so the durable control is a least-privilege permission layer around the agent's tools.

caption:
NVIDIA released Nemotron 3 Ultra, an open 550 billion parameter model with 55 billion active per token. It is a hybrid Mamba-Transformer mixture of experts, it holds up to a million tokens of context, and it is purpose-built for long-running agents that plan and call tools across many turns. The weights are open under a permissive license, so anyone can self-host it. On OpenRouter it is free through June 18 if you pick the :free variant.

The security story is the length. A long-running agent keeps a giant context across hundreds of tool-calling turns, and that context is a durable target. One planted instruction in a fetched page, a document, or a tool's output can sit quietly and steer the agent later. That is indirect prompt injection and context poisoning, stretched over a long session. Open weights also mean there is no vendor moderation, no rate limit, and no kill switch standing behind the model. And this is not theoretical. In its first week on OpenRouter's free tier it pulled tens of billions of tokens a day, peaking over 100 billion prompt tokens, and the most-used public app on it was an autonomous agent, Nous Research's Hermes.

So put the control where you actually own it, outside the model. Require human or policy approval for irreversible and high-impact tool calls. Log the agent's stated goal every turn so goal drift is something you can see. Treat the growing context window as untrusted input, not trusted memory.

Built-in guardrails are optional when the weights are open. The permission layer around your tools is the control that holds.

How long would you let an agent run before a human checks its goal?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: How long would you let an autonomous agent run before a human reviews its goal?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): an immense stone golem seated in the dark, dwarfing a tiny human figure before it, with the headline in the lower third.
Slide 2 (context): a single long unbroken thread spooling endlessly across a dark floor into the distance.
Slide 3 (risk): a vast torrent of glowing particles pouring through a single open gate with no keeper beside it into the dark.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a folded note tucked deep inside a long row of identical sealed boxes stretching into shadow, one box glowing.
Slide 5 (failure_point): a row of heavy levers all switched on with no hand on the guard rail, a small ignored tag swinging.
Slide 6 (defense): a single sturdy gate with a closed latch across a narrow path, a lantern beside it.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a heavy iron padlock resting alone on bare stone in deep shadow, a thin rim of light along its edge.
Slide 8 (cta): an hourglass standing in empty dark space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.supports: Open 550B total / 55B active mixture-of-experts hybrid Mamba-Transformer, up to 1M token context, OpenMDW-1.1 license, long-running agent positioning.
Mixture-of-experts hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture aimed at multi-step tool use and long-horizon agents.

sources.source: MarkTechPost: NVIDIA AI Releases Nemotron 3 Ultra
sources.supports: Public release on June 4, 2026 of an open 550B MoE hybrid Mamba-Transformer for long-running agents.

sources.source: NVIDIA Developer Blog: Nemotron 3 Ultra Powers Faster, More Efficient Reasoning for Long-Running Agents
sources.supports: NVIDIA positions Nemotron 3 Ultra for efficient long-horizon reasoning and multi-step tool use in long-running agents.

sources.source: Nous Research: Run Nemotron 3 Ultra Free (guide)
sources.supports: The nvidia/nemotron-3-ultra:free tier is available June 4 to June 18, 2026; the :free tag keeps it on the no-cost plan.

sources.source: OpenRouter: Nemotron 3 Ultra (free) activity and top apps
sources.supports: OpenRouter usage shows tens of billions of tokens per day in the model's first week (prompt usage peaking past 100 billion in a day), with autonomous and coding agents (Hermes Agent ranked first, then Kilo Code, OpenClaw, Claude Code, Cline) as the top public apps.

narration.text: NVIDIA just open sourced a 550 billion parameter model built to run agents for hours at a time.
Nemotron 3 Ultra holds up to a million tokens of context, the weights are open, and agents are already pushing tens of billions of tokens a day through it.
So put the control outside the model. Require human approval for high-impact tool calls, and log the agent's goal every turn so you can catch drift.
Built-in guardrails are optional when the weights are open. The permission layer around your tools is the control that actually holds.
How long would you let an agent run before a human checks in? Tell me below.
